Software Development

Software Engineering

Agency

Web Development

Startup

3 min read
3 hours ago

--

Launching a website is exciting.
It’s also the moment when small mistakes become public.
Over the past few projects, I’ve started following the same checklist before pressing “Publish.” It helps me catch issues that are easy to miss after staring at the same website for days.
This isn’t the only way to launch a website, but it’s the process I use today — and I’ll keep improving it as I gain more experience.

1. Check the Domain & Hosting
Before anything else, I make sure the basics are solid.
For domains, I usually choose Namecheap. It has been reliable for me and offers reasonable pricing.
For hosting, I currently prefer Cloudflare because it provides security out of the box and doesn’t come with many of the limitations I’ve experienced elsewhere.
I also recommend using a professional email address. It immediately makes a business look more trustworthy.
Personally, I use Zoho Mail because it fits my budget, but if you’re comparing providers, I’d suggest researching alternatives as well to find the one that best matches your needs.

2. Make Sure the Website Loads Quickly
People won’t wait forever.
My personal goal is for a website to load in under five seconds, although faster is always better.
The easiest improvements usually come from:

• Optimizing images

• Reducing unnecessary JavaScript

• Loading only the resources you actually need

• Keeping videos and other large assets lightweight
Performance isn’t just about SEO.
It’s about respecting your visitors’ time.

3. Test the Mobile Experience
Most visitors will probably see your website on a phone before they ever open it on a desktop.
I usually test using desktop previews and an iPhone before launch.
The problems I look for include:

• Broken burger menus

• Overflow issues

• Buttons that are difficult to tap

• Elements overlapping each other

• Sections that don’t scale correctly
A beautiful desktop design means very little if the mobile version frustrates users.

4. Ask Yourself: Does This Really Need Another Page?
This is one of my strongest opinions.
For many small businesses, a well-structured landing page is enough.
I often see websites with five or six pages that could have comfortably fit into one clear, scrollable experience.
More pages don’t automatically make a better website.
Sometimes they just make visitors click more.

5. Make Contact Effortless
If someone decides they want to become your customer, don’t make them search for a way to reach you.
I always try to include:

• Email

• A contact form

• Calendar booking (when appropriate)
Removing friction often matters more than adding features.

6. Build Trust Before Asking for Action
People buy from businesses they trust.
Some of the strongest trust signals include:

• Real portfolio examples

• Genuine client reviews

• Photos of your work

• Showing the people behind the business
In the age of AI-generated content, authenticity has become even more valuable.
Showing the humans behind the brand is often a competitive advantage.

7. Install Analytics From Day One
A website shouldn’t just look good.
It should teach you something.
I always recommend connecting Google Analytics before launch.
Without analytics, you’re making decisions based on assumptions instead of real visitor behavior.

8. Do One Final Walkthrough
Right before publishing, I go through a simple mental checklist:

• Does the website open correctly?

• Are all the latest changes deployed?

• Did anything accidentally break?

• Are forms working?

• Are there any grammar or spelling mistakes?
If something feels risky, I’d rather hide it temporarily than launch a broken feature.

Final Thoughts
Publishing a website isn’t the finish line.
It’s the beginning.
The real feedback starts when actual people visit your website, click your buttons, fill out your forms, and interact with your business.
That’s why I treat every launch as version 1.0, not the final version.
Every website can be improved.
The important thing is launching with confidence — and continuing to make it better.
